title: Nutritional Support for the Preterm Infant
authors: Brenda B. Poindexter & Camilia R. Martin / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Nutritional Support for the Preterm Infant?
โภชนาการไม่ใช่แค่ 'เรื่องของพยาบาล' — extreme prematurity should be viewed as a nutritional emergency (Poindexter & Martin 2025). ทารก ELBW มี glucose store เพียง 200 kcal และสูญเสีย 1% ของ body protein ต่อวันถ้าได้รับแค่ IV glucose. การสะสม cumulative protein and energy deficits ใน 2–3 สัปดาห์แรกเป็น predictor สำคัญที่สุดของ postnatal growth failure และ neurodevelopmental impairment ตลอดชีวิต. Ehrenkranz Pediatrics 1999: ทารก ELBW ที่ gain weight 21 g/kg/day vs 12 g/kg/day มีความเสี่ยง cerebral palsy, MDI <70, และ neurodevelopmental impairment ต่างกันอย่างมีนัยสำคัญ. เรียนวันนี้ = รู้ตัวเลขทุกตัว เพื่อ order TPN และ enteral feeds ได้แบบ precision nutrition.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย postnatal growth curves ที่ใช้ใน NICU: Fenton, Olsen, VON, INTERGROWTH-21st — ข้อจำกัดและการใช้งาน
ระบุ protein requirements ของ preterm <1200 g: 4.0 g/kg/day (factorial method); parenteral max 3.5 g/kg/day; conditionally essential amino acids
กำหนด energy requirement สำหรับ enterally fed VLBW: 110–130 kcal/kg/day; อธิบาย energy expenditure components
อธิบาย lipid requirements: DHA 0.5–1% total fatty acids (≈30–65 mg/kg/day); ARA:DHA ratio 1:0.5; LCPUFA postnatal deficits
สั่ง early parenteral nutrition: AA 2.5–3.0 g/kg/day, lipid 3 g/kg/day วันแรก; glucose GIR starting 3–6 mg/kg/min (ELBW)
อธิบาย trophic feeding: ประโยชน์, timing, rate of advancement 20–30 mL/kg/day (VLBW) vs 15–25 mL/kg/day (ELBW)
ระบุ evidence re: gastric residuals (ไม่แนะนำ routine measurement) และ standardized feeding protocols (RR NEC 0.22)
เปรียบเทียบ lipid emulsions: Intralipid vs Smoflipid vs Omegaven — composition, indications, evidence
อธิบาย human milk fortification: เมื่อไหร่ เท่าไหร่ protein declining curve; bovine vs human milk-based fortifiers
ระบุ electrolytes, minerals, trace elements, vitamins ที่สำคัญ: sodium late onset, calcium/phosphorus PN solubility, iron 2–4 wk, zinc 400 μg/kg/day PN
อธิบาย PNALD mechanism, PN complications, และ refeeding syndrome in ELBW
1. Growth Monitoring in the NICU
1.1 Growth Curves และข้อจำกัด

Fenton 2013: international cohort ~4 million infants จาก 6 population-based studies; available online; incorporated in EMR platforms. Limitation: generated from measurements POST premature birth — ไม่ใช่ in utero physiologic state

Olsen curves: >250,000 infants US born 22–41 wk; combined with WHO/CDC growth charts → monitor postnatal growth ถึง 50 wk corrected age (pediatrix.com). Limitation: <1% of cohort at 22–24 wk → limited generalizability in periviable range
VON curves: 156,000 infants from 852 US centers; includes more periviable infants: 2,000 at 22 wk, 9,000 at 23 wk — better for <24 wk (Boghossian Pediatrics 2016)

INTERGROWTH-21st: serial intrauterine ultrasound measurements — potentially overcomes birth-derived curve limitations. Salas Pediatr Res 2021: current definitions of growth failure (weight-based) not reliable predictors of cognitive impairment (Bayley-III <85) → better definitions needed
Tracking metric: weight at 36–40 wk PMA = practical QI metric; but weight alone insufficient — linear growth and head circumference must be tracked concurrently (Pfister Clin Perinatol 2014)
1.2 Growth and Neurodevelopmental Outcomes

Ehrenkranz Landmark Study — In-Hospital Weight Gain and NDO
Ehrenkranz RA et al Pediatrics 1999;104:280 — n=600 ELBW infants: divided into quartiles by in-hospital growth velocity. Highest quartile: gained 21 g/kg/day. Lowest quartile: 12 g/kg/day. Compared to highest, lowest quartile had significantly higher odds of: cerebral palsy (OR signif.), BSID-II MDI <70, neurodevelopmental impairment. Similar findings for in-hospital head growth. Key message: nutritional delivery modifies disease risk — ไม่ใช่แค่ 'ให้อิ่ม' แต่ quality of growth matters. Cumulative deficits in first weeks (Embleton Pediatrics 2001) are difficult to recoup ใน extremely premature infants.

Fenton TR et al J Perinatol 2020: terms 'extrauterine growth restriction' และ 'postnatal growth failure' = misnomers — focused on arbitrary statistical cut-offs; do not reliably predict adverse outcomes; focus on weight not proportionality. แต่ทำ QI tracking ยังมีประโยชน์
2. Nutrient Requirements — ตัวเลขที่ต้องรู้จำ
2.1 Protein Requirements

Protein — สรุปตัวเลขสำคัญ
Fetal accretion at 26 wk: 1.8–2.2 g protein/day; placenta supplies 3.5 g/kg/day amino acids to fetus (excess oxidized for energy). Without IV AA: ELBW loses ~1.2 g/kg/day protein = 1–2% of body protein stores/day = 22% body protein lost in 1 week. Factorial method requirement <1200 g: 4.0 g/kg/day. Expert consensus VLBW: 3.5–4.5 g/kg/day enterally; max 3.5 g/kg/day parenterally (van den Akker World Rev Nutr Diet 2021). Exceeding 3.5 g/kg/day IV: risks acidemia, refeeding-hypophosphatemia syndrome, adverse NDO speech/language (Moon 2021; Blanco 2011). Achieving 3.5–4.5 g/kg/day enterally is challenging — 'high-protein' preterm formula provides 4.0 g/kg/day at 150 mL/kg/day but unfortified HM only 1.8–2.1 g/kg/day.
Conditionally Essential Amino Acids ใน Preterm

Tyrosine: low solubility → absent in most AA solutions. Synthesized from phenylalanine by phenylalanine hydroxylase — may be inadequate in premature infants. N-acetyltyrosine (in TrophAmine) — low bioavailability. Can cause transient neonatal tyrosinemia if catabolism impaired

Cysteine: absent in AA solutions (unstable in aqueous solution). Can add cysteine hydrochloride during compounding — improves N retention AND improves calcium/phosphorus solubility. Important: cysteine HCl → metabolic acidosis unless buffered with acetate

Glutamine: most abundant amino acid in plasma + human milk; absent from AA solutions (unstable). Large NICHD multicenter RCT: parenteral glutamine did NOT decrease mortality or late-onset sepsis in ELBW infants → routine supplementation not supported
Taurine: present in TrophAmine/Primene/Aminosyn-PF at 1–3× human milk levels. Limited evidence it influences auditory brainstem-evoked responses. Endogenously synthesized from cysteine
Arginine: conditionally essential; deficiency may contribute to NEC pathogenesis — potential supplementation benefit under study
2.2 Energy Requirements
Energy expenditure: 50–60 kcal/kg/day (ventilated infants — limited data). Thermal stress ↑↑ expenditure. Physical activity small contributor in preterm
Energy cost of growth: ~5 kcal/g of new tissue. To gain 14–18 g/kg/day (third trimester rate): need extra 70 kcal/kg/day on top of maintenance
Recommended enteral intake (VLBW): 110–130 kcal/kg/day (Koletzko World Rev Nutr Diet 2021). Assumes adequate protein. SGA/BPD may need more

Parenteral caloric needs MUCH lower than enteral (no intestinal losses, no diet-induced thermogenesis): 80–90 kcal/kg/day nonprotein energy for ELBW on PN. Providing excessive PN calories does NOT correlate with higher growth rates
Nonprotein caloric balance: carbohydrate:lipid ≈ 60:40 for maximal protein retention and minimal excess energy expenditure (mimics breast milk fat content)
2.3 Carbohydrates

Glucose production rate: term: 3–5 mg/kg/min; ELBW: 8–9 mg/kg/min (higher per kg due to larger brain:body ratio). Starting GIR: 3–6 mg/kg/min for <1000 g; advance to 10–12 mg/kg/min if no hyperglycemia
Hyperglycemia definition: plasma >150 mg/dL (8.3 mmol/L). Levels <200 mg/dL usually no intervention. Glucose concentrate >12.5% → central access only

Insulin NOT recommended routinely: Beardsall NEJM 2008: early insulin therapy → no difference in mortality; may ↑ hypoglycemia episodes (trial terminated early for futility). Occasional use: 0.05–0.1 U/kg/h for hyperglycemia unresponsive to ↓ GIR

Lactose in human milk: 6.2–7.2 g/dL (40–50% calories); efficiently digested despite low lactase — stable isotope data. Preterm formulas: glucose polymers (40–50% carbohydrate) — digested by α-glucosidases (70% adult activity by 26–34 wk), salivary + mammary amylases
2.4 Lipid Requirements and LCPUFAs

DHA and ARA — Postnatal Deficits and Recommendations
In utero: DHA and ARA supplied via placenta. After birth: preterm infants develop postnatal DHA and ARA deficits within first week (Martin JPed 2011) — lack of adipose reserves + early nutritional strategies. Even Smoflipid (which contains DHA) fails to correct: minimal DHA change, ↓ ARA further, ↑ EPA substantially. ARA decline risk: ↑ late-onset sepsis + retinopathy of prematurity (Löfqvist JAMA Ophthalmol 2018). Current recommendation VLBW: DHA intake = 0.5–1% total fatty acids ≈ 30–65 mg/kg/day. ARA:DHA ratio ≈ 2:1 (closer to 0.5 DHA:ARA) (Koletzko World Rev Nutr Diet 2021; clinical + animal data support ratio closer to 0.5 DHA:ARA — twice ARA to DHA).

Fat digestion in preterm: low pancreatic lipase + bile acids → compensated by intragastric lipolysis (lingual lipase from 25 wk, gastric lipase). Lingual lipase penetrates HM lipid globule without disrupting membrane. Unpasteurized HM provides lipoprotein lipase, bile salt-stimulated esterase → aids lipolysis

MCT in preterm formula: 20–50% — absorbed without lipase/bile acids, transported via portal vein, enter mitochondria without carnitine. But: available data do NOT support improved fat absorption or growth vs LCT (Simmer Cochrane 2005)

Carnitine: mediates long-chain fatty acid transport through mitochondrial membrane. Preterm PN → low carnitine levels. Clinical significance uncertain. Added to HM and preterm formulas above breast milk levels
3. Parenteral Nutrition — Early and Aggressive
3.1 Initiation Protocol

PN Day 1 — อย่ารอ
ELBW = nutritional emergency: glucose stores 200 kcal; loses 1% body protein/day without AA. Start IV amino acids IMMEDIATELY after birth: 2.5–3.0 g/kg/day. Many NICUs use 'neonatal starter' AA solution (TrophAmine/Premasol) for rapid availability. IV lipids: start at 3 g/kg/day from day 1 of full PN — no need for stepwise increase (evidence: Simmer Cochrane 2005; no ↑ chronic lung disease). Glucose: start 3–6 mg/kg/min (ELBW) → advance to 10–12 mg/kg/min if tolerating. Enteral feeds (EBM) = initiate as soon as possible. Critical studies: Thureen Pediatr Res 2003 — 3 g/kg/day vs 1 g/kg/day AA from birth → significantly greater protein accretion with high dose; te Braake J Pediatr 2005 — AA from day 1 vs glucose alone day 1 → positive N balance vs negative N balance.
3.2 Intravenous Amino Acid Solutions

Current solutions: TrophAmine / Premasol — formulated to match plasma AA of healthy term breastfed infants; supply L-tyrosine + N-acetyltyrosine (low bioavailability concern). Primene (outside US) — derived from fetal/neonatal cord blood concentrations; Aminosyn-PF — no substantial tyrosine. None designed specifically for ELBW; none modified >25 years

BUN monitoring: generally BUN not correlated with AA dose in first days, but 2 studies showed ↑ BUN at 7 days with higher AA dose (Clark Pediatrics 2007; Blanco J Pediatr 2008). Azotemia may reflect effective AA oxidation as energy — not necessarily intolerance

Combined protein + energy: protein quantity = primary determinant of protein accretion. At constant N intake: ↑ nonprotein energy 50 → 80 kcal/kg/day → ↑ N retention + weight gain. But at low energy (50 kcal/kg/day): ↑ AA 3 → 4 g/kg/day had NO effect on N retention
3.3 Intravenous Fat Emulsions

Smoflipid vs Intralipid: meta-analyses do NOT demonstrate improved neonatal outcomes including PNALD reduction (Hojsak J Pediatr Gastroenterol Nutr 2016; Kapoor Cochrane 2015). Large Phase 3 trial NCT02579265 terminated for nonsuperiority in 2020 (interim analysis) — final manuscript not published. Routine Smoflipid preference over Intralipid: not evidence-based for PNALD prevention

Omegaven (fish oil 100%): multiple studies/case reports in infants → improvement in hepatic function + reversal of PNALD. FDA 2018 for PNALD management. Use when conjugated bilirubin ↑ on PN + unable to advance enteral feeds

Lipid infusion rate: >0.25 g/kg/h → ↓ oxygenation. Provide 3–4 g/kg/day over 24 hours continuous infusion. Maintain triglycerides <200 mg/dL. Heparin to stimulate lipolysis: NOT recommended (no evidence of ↑ lipid utilization; may produce excess free fatty acids)
4. Enteral Nutrition — Strategy and Advancement
4.1 Trophic Feeding (Minimal Enteral Feeding)
Definition: typically ≤25 mL/kg/day; duration usually 1–4 days (varies by institution)
Benefits: gut maturation (structural + functional), ↓ liver dysfunction, ↑ feeding tolerance, ↓ NEC onset and progression to surgical NEC (Bozzetti World Rev Nutr Diet 2021)
Cochrane systematic review (Morgan 2013): early trophic feeding (<96h) vs fasting in VLBW: no difference in feeding tolerance, growth, or NEC. ✓ safe to start trophic feeds early
IUGR infants: Leaf Pediatrics 2012 (RCT): early vs delayed initiation in preterm IUGR → no evidence of ↑ NEC with early initiation; no benefit to delaying beyond 24–48h
4.2 Rate of Advancement

SIFT Trial — Fast vs Slow Advancement
SIFT (Speed of Increasing Milk Feedings) — Dorling NEJM 2019;381:1434: large multicenter UK RCT. Randomized to faster (30 mL/kg/day) vs slower advancement of enteral feeds in preterm infants. Result: no increase in NEC incidence with faster advancement; no difference in survival without moderate/severe NDO. Clinical implication: rapid advancement ≠ ↑ NEC risk. Reasonable approach: advance 20–30 mL/kg/day in VLBW infants; 15–25 mL/kg/day in ELBW infants. Future studies needed for ELBW-specific optimal rate.
4.3 Gastric Residuals

Evidence: NO support for routine measurement of gastric residuals to diagnose feeding intolerance or NEC. In the absence of other symptoms: discontinuing feeds based solely on gastric residuals is NOT evidence-based (Kaplan World Rev Nutr Diet 2021)
Not measuring gastric residuals = element of unit-based standardized feeding protocols → reduces variation in practice
4.4 Standardized Feeding Guidelines

Standardized Feeding Protocols — RR NEC 0.22
Jasani & Patole J Perinatol 2017: systematic review; standardized feeding protocols reduced NEC incidence with RR 0.22 — regardless of protocol content. แสดงว่า consistency ของ practice สำคัญกว่า specific protocol details. Additional benefits: ↓ time to full enteral feeds, ↓ days on PN, ↑ growth. Common controversies: feeding with umbilical arterial catheter, inotropic support, during packed RBC transfusion, hemodynamically significant PDA — insufficient evidence for clear recommendation (Martini Nutrients 2019); standardizing approach within unit reduces variation.
4.5 Enteral Feeds in Specific Clinical Conditions
Post-NEC Feeding

After medical management of NEC: similar approach to original initiation — prioritize unpasteurized maternal HM → donor milk → formula if insufficient. Hydrolyzed protein formula considers overlap with FPIES (food protein-induced enterocolitis syndrome — mimics NEC, Kim Ann Allergy 2022; Lenfestey J Pediatr 2018)
Timing of resumption post-NEC remains active area of study — no definitive RCT data
Therapeutic Hypothermia

Wide practice variation in enteral feeding during cooling (Hazeldine BMJ Paediatr Open 2017). Recent data suggest initiation of enteral feeding during TH is SAFE and should be considered (Gale Lancet Child Adolesc Health 2021; Hu Ital J Pediatr 2022 — RCT: early vs delayed EN during TH → early safe)
5. Human Milk, Donor Milk, and Fortification
5.1 Human Milk — ทำไมสำคัญที่สุด
NEC protection: dose-dependent (Meinzen-Derr J Perinatol 2009)
NDO benefit: Vohr BR et al Pediatrics 2006 (NICHD NRN); Vohr Pediatrics 2007: dose-dependent improvement in NDO at 18 months and 30 months in ELBW infants. Benefits persist at 30 months of age
Equity imperative: addressing racial/ethnic disparities in mother's own milk provision = key NICU QI metric (Parker Pediatrics 2021 — AAP policy)
5.2 Donor Human Milk
AAP recommendation: pasteurized donor HM when mother's own milk unavailable (Parker Pediatrics 2021)

DoMINO Trial — O'Connor et al JAMA 2016;316:1897: 363 VLBW infants, donor HM vs preterm formula as supplemental diet to maternal milk. No statistically significant difference in BSID-III cognitive score, but infants randomized to donor milk more likely to score <85 on BSID-III — nuanced result

Donor milk protein content: markedly lower than preterm maternal milk — some pooled donor milk <1 g/dL protein. HM fortifiers assume 1.4–1.6 g/dL → actual protein delivery underestimated as lactation progresses (preterm milk declines from ~1.7 g/dL at 7 days to ~1.2 g/dL by 42 days). Strategies: pre-emptive ↑ macronutrients in donor milk (Fu Nutrients 2019; 2021)
5.3 Human Milk Fortification

HM alone is insufficient: lacks adequate protein, calcium, phosphorus, sodium, zinc, vitamins for ELBW. Multicomponent fortification: improves weight gain, linear growth, head circumference (Kuschel Cochrane 2004)

Timing of fortification: can start as early as enteral intake >50 mL/kg/day (before full feeds). Adjustable fortification (incremental protein guided by BUN) — individualized approach (Arslanoglu EMBA Front Pediatr 2019)

Bovine vs human milk-based fortifier: Sullivan J Pediatr 2010 (small multicenter RCT): exclusive HM diet → lower NEC/death than bovine fortifier group. But: no difference in primary outcome (duration PN); no head-to-head well-designed trial; current evidence supports bovine protein-based multicomponent fortifier (Picaud World Rev Nutr Diet 2021)
Powdered fortifiers: avoid — ↑ risk infectious complications
6. Preterm Formulas and Specialized Formulas
6.1 Preterm Formulas

Preterm formula features: ↓ lactose (40–50%); glucose polymers maintain low osmolality (≤300 mOsm); MCT 20–50% fat blend; ↑ Ca/P for bone mineralization; whey-predominant (↓ lactobezoar risk; optimal AA profile)
6.2 Specialized Formulas
For malabsorption/intolerance: lactose-free, cow milk protein-free; protein sources: soy, casein hydrolysates, free amino acids; significant MCT percentage

Vitamin/mineral content similar to term formula → supplementation needed for preterm vitamin/mineral targets. Use only temporarily; reinstitute standard preterm feeds as tolerated. Not tested in premature infants
7. Electrolytes, Minerals, Trace Elements, Vitamins
7.1 Electrolytes

Sodium: first week: low needs (free water diuresis — may omit Na from PN day 1–2 in ELBW). After diuresis: 2–4 mEq/kg/day. ELBW may need 3–5 mEq/kg/day → match intrauterine growth rate. Late-onset hyponatremia: HM/fortifiers provide only 2–3 mEq/kg/day at full feeds — may not prevent (Göke J Matern Fetal Neonatal Med 2020). Urine sodium levels: better proxy for total body sodium than serum Na (Segar Am J Perinatol 2018) — adjust supplementation by urine Na
Potassium: omit from PN until renal function established. Then 2–3 mEq/kg/day adequate
PN anions: excess as acetate to prevent hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis; especially important when cysteine HCl added
7.2 Calcium and Phosphorus
Peak mineral accretion: third trimester → preterm at ↑↑ risk osteopenia

PN Ca/P solubility limited: depends on: temperature, AA concentration, glucose concentration, pH, type of Ca salt, sequence of addition, Ca:P ratio, lipid presence. Adding cysteine HCl → ↓ pH → ↑ Ca/P solubility
Enteral recommendations: Ca 120–220 mg/kg/day; P 70–120 mg/kg/day (Taylor World Rev Nutr Diet 2021)

Metabolic bone disease: diuretics + corticosteroids further ↓ bone mineralization. Unfortified HM post-discharge → bone mineral deficits persisting through 52 wk (Kuschel 2004). Screen ALP, phosphate in ELBW; DXA scan if clinically indicated
7.3 Zinc and Trace Elements

Zinc PN: 400 μg/kg/day for preterm infants; zinc sulfate added separately. Enteral: up to 3 μg/kg/day recommended (Domellöf World Rev Nutr Diet 2021). Meta-analyses suggest enteral zinc ↑ growth; high-dose zinc may ↓ NEC/mortality (Terrin Am J Clin Nutr 2013; Staub Cochrane 2021 — further studies needed)
Selenium: supplement after 2 wk (risk of deficiency on exclusive PN). Copper + manganese: discontinue if cholestasis. Chromium + selenium: use with caution in renal dysfunction
Pediatric trace metal solutions (zinc, copper, Mg, chromium): 0.2 mL/kg/day; additional zinc usually needed. Abnormal GI losses (diarrhea/ileostomy) → supplement zinc + electrolytes extra
7.4 Iron
Enteral iron: 2–3 mg/kg/day inclusive of iron in formula/fortifier. Start at 2–4 weeks of age (Domellöf). Parenteral iron NOT routinely recommended
Erythropoietin/darbepoetin → higher iron dose needed
Serum ferritin: if ferritin >300 μg/L → hold supplementation until normalizes. Excessive iron → ↑ sepsis risk + poor growth
Phlebotomy + transfusions: dramatically alter iron stores — ferritin monitoring guides dosing in high-transfusion infants; delayed cord clamping affects initial stores
7.5 Vitamins
Vitamin D: total 400–1000 IU/day (inclusive of milk + supplement) for enterally fed preterm infants (Koletzko 2021)
Vitamin E: 3–4 IU/day (term); higher for preterm. High dose 100 mg/kg/day NOT recommended to reduce ROP, BPD, IVH (no proven benefit)
Vitamin K: 1 mg IM within 6h of birth for >1500 g; 0.3–0.5 mg/kg for ≤1500 g. Then 2–3 mg/kg/day or 5–10 mg daily in diet

Water-soluble vitamins: daily intake required (no storage). Renal immaturity → altered urinary losses in first week → ↑ vitamin C loss. Liquid multivitamin drops: no folic acid (unstable) → add separately
8. Complications of Parenteral Nutrition
8.1 PNALD — Parenteral Nutrition-Associated Liver Disease

Manifestation: cholestatic jaundice → portal inflammation → bile duct proliferation → portal fibrosis → cirrhosis. Risk factors: duration of PN, degree of immaturity, delayed enteral feeding (most important). Phytosterol content of soybean-based lipids implicated

Prevention: early enteral feeds (even small-volume trophic feeds → ↓ cholestasis); ↓ duration of PN; maintain enteral stimulation. Omegaven (FDA 2018): consistent improvement in PNALD across multiple studies/case reports in infants → use when PNALD established and enteral advancement limited
8.2 Central Line Complications

CLABSI: S. epidermidis and S. aureus (bacterial); Candida albicans, Malassezia furfur (fungal). Incidence ↑ with ↓ GA and ↑ PN duration. Lipid emulsion provides rich growth medium for Malassezia in catheter. QI initiatives (CLABSI bundle) dramatically reduce infection rate

Light protection of lipid: phototherapy may ↑ peroxidation in IV lipid emulsions. Multivitamins in PN are larger contributor to peroxide generation than lipids. Efficacy of light-covering lipids: unclear — some centers do it
8.3 Neonatal Refeeding Syndrome

Refeeding Syndrome ใน ELBW — Cormack et al ProVIDe Trial 2021
Cormack BE et al JPEN 2021;45:65 (ProVIDe secondary analysis): high-protein, high-energy PN solutions early after birth WITHOUT electrolytes → neonatal refeeding syndrome: ↓ phosphate, ↓ potassium, ↓ magnesium. Clinical presentation: sepsis-like syndrome + ↑ mortality risk. Prevention: early electrolyte monitoring including phosphate; include phosphate in PN from outset; avoid prolonging PN unnecessarily. Metabolic bone disease: ↑ risk with prolonged PN (Perrone Int J Mol Sci 2022) — screen with serial ALP + phosphate.
9. Post-Discharge Nutrition

Nutrient needs at discharge > term infant needs: VLBW discharged before meeting in utero mineral + protein needs. HM fortification post-discharge: feasible without compromising breastfeeding success; benefits include ↑ weight, linear growth, possibly vision + lung function (Koletzko World Rev Nutr Diet 2021)

Post-discharge formula (nutrient-enriched): expert groups recommend for formula-fed preterm infants (higher protein, minerals, trace elements, LCPUFA). Cochrane (Young 2016): nutrient-enriched formula vs term formula post-discharge → no significant difference in growth outcomes. BUT preterm formula post-discharge may improve growth at 18 months corrected age (Young Cochrane 2012). Further research needed
10. Controversies and Areas of Active Study
10.1 Catch-Up Growth and Metabolic Disease (Barker Hypothesis)

Barker hypothesis concerns: rapid catch-up growth → ↑ risk of later metabolic disease (hypertension, insulin resistance). Literature mixed re: balance of growth and later metabolic health — varies by degree of maturity and long-term follow-up cohorts
Belfort MB et al Pediatrics 2010;125:e1419: former preterm infants — infant weight gain associated with school-age blood pressure and cognition. Balanced approach needed
Body composition tools: improving standardization of fat vs lean mass assessment → better insights into which types of growth correlate with long-term outcomes (Demerath Am J Clin Nutr 2017)
10.2 Transfusion-Associated NEC (TANEC)
Cohort studies suggested NEC within 48h of transfusion ('TANEC'). But:

Multiple meta-analyses: no significant association; large RCTs comparing restrictive vs liberal transfusion policies: no association. Current view: transfusion = proxy for other acute illness elements (degree of anemia) rather than direct NEC cause
Avoid unnecessary holding of feeds for non-GI concerns (PDA, transfusion, inotropes) without clear evidence — interruption of nutrition has its own costs
11. NICU Practical — Nutrition Order Set Thinking
11.1 Day 1 ELBW Checklist
Protein: start TrophAmine/Premasol 2.5–3.0 g/kg/day immediately; add cysteine HCl (↑ Ca/P solubility + ↑ N retention); buffer with acetate to prevent acidosis
Lipid: start Intralipid 3 g/kg/day over 24 hours; monitor TG <200 mg/dL; light cover optional (unclear benefit)
Glucose: GIR 3–6 mg/kg/min (5% dextrose); advance if tolerating. Target plasma glucose 50–150 mg/dL. No routine insulin
Electrolytes: omit Na/K day 1 (diuresis phase); add phosphate from day 1 (refeeding prevention); Ca:P ratio ≈ 1.3:1 mmol
Enteral: start EBM trophic 25 mL/kg/day as soon as stable; advance 15–25 mL/kg/day daily in ELBW; no routine gastric residual checks
Monitoring: daily: weight, glucose, electrolytes including phosphate; weekly: TG, LFTs, Ca/P/ALP/ferritin (if prolonged PN)
11.2 เมื่อ Fortification สำคัญที่สุด

Protein assumption gap: HM fortifier assumes 1.4–1.6 g/dL protein but actual preterm HM declines to 1.2 g/dL by 42 days — donor HM may be <1 g/dL. ผล: ทารก ELBW ที่ feed fortified HM อาจยังขาด protein โดยที่เราไม่รู้
Adjustable fortification: target BUN 10–20 mg/dL เมื่อ on full enteral feeds with fortifier; ถ้า BUN <10 → เพิ่ม protein; ถ้า BUN >25 พร้อม metabolic acidosis → ลด protein
Sodium monitoring: check urine sodium (uNa) weekly; uNa <20 mEq/L = depleted → supplement 3–5 mEq/kg/day orally
12. Drug/Nutrient Reference Table

rows: Max 3.5 g/kg/day parenteral; add cysteine HCl
Start day 1; TG <200 mg/dL; max 4 g/kg/day
Add separately to PN; extra zinc above trace metal solution
Inclusive of fortifier iron; hold if ferritin >300 μg/L

blocks.text: 13. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — ELBW คือ Nutritional Emergency — PN วันแรก ไม่ใช่วันที่สาม
ทารก ELBW มี glucose 200 kcal และสูญเสีย protein 1%/day ถ้าได้แค่ glucose. Embleton Pediatrics 2001: cumulative protein deficit สัปดาห์แรก impossible to recoup. te Braake J Pediatr 2005: AA ตั้งแต่ day 1 → positive N balance; delay → negative N balance day 2. Protocol ที่ถูกต้อง: เปิด PN พร้อม TrophAmine 2.5–3.0 g/kg/day + Intralipid 3 g/kg/day ตั้งแต่ admit; เริ่ม trophic EBM ทันที. อย่าให้ 'รอ stable' นาน — ทุก 24h delay = 1.2 g/kg protein loss. Neonatologist ที่ดีคือคนที่ order PN เป็นลำดับแรกสุดหลัง admit.

PEARL 2 — Gastric Residuals ไม่มีหลักฐาน — อย่าหยุด Feed เพราะ GR เดียว
มีหลักฐาน? ไม่มี (Kaplan World Rev Nutr Diet 2021). การ routine check GR และหยุด feeds = ทำให้ cumulative nutritional deficit ↑↑ โดยไม่จำเป็น. Standardized protocols ที่ไม่ routine check GR ลด NEC (Jasani J Perinatol 2017 RR 0.22) และเพิ่ม days on full feeds. ข้อยกเว้นที่ควรพิจารณา: GR bilious/bloody, ท้องอืดมาก, clinical deterioration, HA absent bowel sound — เหล่านี้ไม่ใช่ GR เดียวๆ. อย่าหยุด feed เพราะ 3 mL GR สีเหลืองในทารก 800g ที่ healthy.

PEARL 3 — Protein Gap ใน HM Fortification — ตัวเลขที่หลายคนไม่รู้
HM fortifier assumes 1.4–1.6 g/dL protein BUT: preterm HM จริงๆ เริ่มจาก ~1.7 g/dL day 7 และ ลดลงเหลือ ~1.2 g/dL ที่ 42 วัน; donor HM อาจ <1 g/dL. ดังนั้น ทารก ELBW สัปดาห์ที่ 4–6 ที่กิน fortified HM 150 mL/kg/day อาจได้ protein แค่ 2.5–3.0 g/kg/day ทั้งที่ target คือ 4.0+ g/kg/day. Solution: adjustable fortification guided by BUN (target 10–20 mg/dL); monitor urine Na หา late-onset hyponatremia; track weight gain rate weekly. ถ้า weight gain <15 g/kg/day → review protein + energy delivery จริงๆ ก่อน assume 'intolerance'.

PEARL 4 — SIFT Trial: เร็ว ≠ NEC — อย่าปรับ Feed ช้าเพราะกลัว
Dorling NEJM 2019: SIFT trial (large multicenter UK RCT): faster advancement (30 mL/kg/day) vs slower → NO increase in NEC; NO difference in survival without severe NDO. ดังนั้น: กลัว NEC ≠ เหตุผลสำหรับ slow advancement. การ advance feeds ช้า ↑ days on PN, ↑ CLABSI risk, ↑ PNALD risk, ↑ cumulative nutrition deficit. ยังคงต้องระวัง ELBW (<1000g): advance 15–25 mL/kg/day; monitor clinical signs (abdomen, bowel sounds, output). แต่ไม่มีเหตุผลทาง evidence ที่จะ advance แค่ 10 mL/kg/day ใน VLBW ที่ stable.

PEARL 5 — Refeeding Syndrome ใน ELBW: Phosphate ตั้งแต่วันแรก
Cormack JPEN 2021 (ProVIDe secondary): high-protein high-energy PN without electrolytes → ↓ phosphate, ↓ K, ↓ Mg → sepsis-like syndrome + ↑ mortality. Mechanism: anabolic stimulus → cellular uptake phosphate → plasma ↓↓. Prevention: include phosphate ใน PN solution ตั้งแต่วันแรก; monitor serum phosphate daily week 1 (monitor อย่างน้อย 3× ต่อสัปดาห์). ถ้า phosphate <1.8 mg/dL → เพิ่ม IV phosphate supplement; พิจารณาลด protein/energy rate ชั่วคราว. อย่าลืมว่า cysteine HCl ↑ Ca/P solubility ใน PN → ใช้ประโยชน์นี้ให้ optimal.
14. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Ehrenkranz RA et al Pediatrics 1999;104:280: n=600 ELBW, quartile in-hospital weight gain — lowest quartile → ↑ CP, MDI <70, NDI; defining study of growth-NDO link
Embleton NE et al Pediatrics 2001;107:270: cumulative nutritional deficits in preterm — protein + energy deficits first weeks difficult to recoup; urges aggressive early nutrition
te Braake FW et al J Pediatr 2005;147:457: AA from birth day 1 vs glucose only → positive N balance day 1 vs negative N balance day 2 in control; no adverse effects
Thureen PJ et al Pediatr Res 2003;53:24: 1 vs 3 g/kg/day IV AA from birth in ELBW — higher dose → significantly greater protein accretion, no adverse effects
Beardsall K et al NEJM 2008;359:1873: early insulin therapy in VLBW — no difference in mortality; ↑ hypoglycemia episodes; trial terminated for futility; routine insulin not recommended
Dorling J et al NEJM 2019;381:1434 (SIFT): faster (30 mL/kg/day) vs slower advancement in preterm — no ↑ NEC; no difference survival without severe NDO
Morgan J et al Cochrane 2013:CD000504: early trophic feeding vs enteral fasting VLBW — no difference NEC, growth, feeding tolerance; supports early initiation
Jasani B & Patole S J Perinatol 2017;37:827: standardized feeding regimens — NEC RR 0.22 regardless of protocol content; ↓ PN days, ↓ time to full feeds

O'Connor DL et al JAMA 2016;316:1897 (DoMINO): 363 VLBW, donor HM vs preterm formula supplement — no significant difference primary BSID-III cognitive score; donor milk group more likely BSID-III <85 — nuanced
Martin CR et al J Pediatr 2011;159:743 (DHA/ARA): postnatal DHA + ARA deficits in premature infants — even Smoflipid does not correct; minimal DHA change, ↓ ARA further
Löfqvist CA et al JAMA Ophthalmol 2018;136:271: low ARA levels associated with ↑ retinopathy of prematurity risk — secondary analysis RCT; ARA:DHA ratio matters
Hojsak I et al J Pediatr Gastroenterol Nutr 2016;62:776: ESPGHAN systematic review — alternative lipid emulsions (Smoflipid) vs soy-only: no improved hepatotoxicity outcomes in infants
NCT02579265 (Smoflipid vs Intralipid PNALD): large Phase 3 RCT — terminated 2020 (interim: sample size for superiority infeasible = nonsuperiority Smoflipid in PNALD prevention)
Sullivan S et al J Pediatr 2010;156:562: exclusive HM diet → ↓ NEC/death vs bovine fortifier group; primary outcome (PN duration) not different; small multicenter RCT
Cormack BE et al JPEN 2021;45:65 (ProVIDe): high-protein PN without electrolytes → neonatal refeeding syndrome (↓ PO4/K/Mg) → sepsis-like + ↑ mortality in ELBW
Leaf A et al Pediatrics 2012;129:e1260: early vs delayed enteral feeding in preterm IUGR — no evidence of ↑ NEC with early initiation; no benefit to delay >24–48h
Koletzko B et al World Rev Nutr Diet 2021;122:191: recommended nutrient intake levels for preterm infants — comprehensive reference for all macro/micronutrients
Vohr BR et al Pediatrics 2006;118:e115; Pediatrics 2007;120:e953: NICHD NRN — dose-dependent NDO benefit of breast milk at 18 months (2006) and 30 months (2007) in ELBW infants

Salas AA et al Pediatr Res 2021;89:1804: postnatal growth 24–26 wk and cognitive outcomes — current weight-based definitions of growth failure not reliable predictor of Bayley-III cognitive impairment
15. Self-Check Q&A
Q: ทำไม ELBW ถือว่าเป็น 'nutritional emergency'? และควร start PN เมื่อไหร่?

ตอบ: ทารก ELBW มี glucose stores เพียง 200 kcal และสูญเสีย ~1.2 g/kg/day ของ protein (1–2% body protein/day) หาก provide glucose alone → สูญเสีย 22% body protein ใน 1 สัปดาห์. Cumulative nutritional deficits ใน 2–3 สัปดาห์แรก recoup ไม่ได้ใน extremely preterm (Embleton 2001). Should start IV AA 2.5–3.0 g/kg/day + lipid 3 g/kg/day IMMEDIATELY after birth — ไม่ใช่รอ 'stable' 2–3 วัน.
Q: Protein requirement สำหรับ preterm <1200 g คือเท่าไหร่ และทำไม parenteral max 3.5 g/kg/day?

ตอบ: Enteral requirement: 3.5–4.5 g/kg/day (van den Akker 2021); factorial method estimate <1200 g = 4.0 g/kg/day. Parenteral max: 3.5 g/kg/day — exceeding: (1) ↑ acidemia; (2) refeeding-hypophosphatemia syndrome; (3) potential adverse NDO speech/language domains (Moon 2021; Blanco 2011). Positive N balance achievable with as little as 1.0–1.5 g/kg/day IV AA (limits catabolism); 3 g/kg/day IV → net protein gain ≈ reference fetus. There are no compelling data to support >3.5 g/kg/day IV.
Q: SIFT trial ตอบคำถามอะไร และ clinical implication คืออะไรสำหรับ NICU ของเรา?

ตอบ: SIFT (Dorling NEJM 2019): large UK multicenter RCT — faster (30 mL/kg/day) vs slower advancement. Result: no increase in NEC; no difference survival without severe NDO. Implication: rapid advancement ≠ ↑ NEC risk — fear of fast advancement is NOT evidence-based. Slower = longer PN, ↑ CLABSI, ↑ PNALD, ↑ nutritional deficit. Reasonable practice: VLBW 20–30 mL/kg/day; ELBW 15–25 mL/kg/day. Hold/slow only for genuine clinical indicators (bilious residuals, distended abdomen, bloody stool, deterioration).
Q: อธิบาย HM protein content decline และวิธี address protein gap ใน clinical practice

ตอบ: Preterm HM protein: ~1.7 g/dL ที่ 7 days → ~1.2 g/dL ที่ 42 days of lactation; donor HM often <1.0 g/dL. HM fortifiers assume 1.4–1.6 g/dL → underdelivery of protein over time. Solution: (1) adjustable fortification: extra protein powder if BUN <10 mg/dL; (2) target urine Na monitoring — supplement if uNa <20; (3) track weight gain weekly — if <15 g/kg/day → calculate actual protein delivery; (4) donor HM: pre-emptively ↑ macronutrients (protein-enriched target pooling).
Q: DHA และ ARA requirements ใน preterm: ตัวเลข, ratio, และทำไม Smoflipid ไม่แก้ปัญหา DHA/ARA deficit?

ตอบ: DHA: 0.5–1% total fatty acids ≈ 30–65 mg/kg/day; ARA:DHA ≈ 2:1 (DHA:ARA ratio 0.5). Smoflipid: contains DHA แต่ Martin J Pediatr 2011 shows minimal DHA change, ↓ ARA further, ↑ EPA substantially. ARA decline = ↑ late-onset sepsis + ↑ ROP (Löfqvist JAMA Ophthalmol 2018). Smoflipid's high EPA displaces ARA in cell membranes — net effect not beneficial for ARA. สรุป: Smoflipid ไม่ใช่ solution สำหรับ LCPUFA deficits ใน preterm.
Q: Refeeding syndrome ใน ELBW เกิดจากอะไร และป้องกันอย่างไร?

ตอบ: Mechanism: high protein/energy PN (anabolic stimulus) → rapid intracellular uptake of phosphate, potassium, magnesium → plasma levels drop → hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia → sepsis-like syndrome + ↑ mortality (Cormack ProVIDe JPEN 2021). Prevention: include phosphate ใน PN solution ตั้งแต่วันแรก; monitor serum phosphate daily week 1; acetate buffering เมื่อ add cysteine HCl; monitor K/Mg. Cysteine HCl → ↑ Ca/P solubility → double benefit.
Q: Intralipid vs Smoflipid vs Omegaven: ใช้ตัวไหน เมื่อไหร่?

ตอบ: Intralipid (100% soybean oil): standard first-line. Smoflipid (soy/MCT/olive/fish oil 15%): meta-analyses no improved neonatal outcomes; large Phase 3 RCT (NCT02579265) terminated — nonsuperiority for PNALD prevention. Routine preference of Smoflipid not evidence-based. Omegaven (100% fish oil): FDA-approved 2018 for PNALD management — consistent evidence of hepatic improvement. Use: established PNALD + unable to advance enteral feeds adequately; NOT routine first-line for all ELBW.
Q: Gastric residuals: ควร routine check ไหม และ guideline ปัจจุบันบอกอะไร?

ตอบ: ไม่มีหลักฐานสนับสนุน routine measurement of gastric residuals (Kaplan World Rev Nutr Diet 2021). ไม่ควรหยุด feeds based solely on GR ในกรณีที่ไม่มี symptoms อื่น. Standardized protocols ที่ไม่ routine check GR → ↓ NEC RR 0.22 (Jasani 2017). Clinical signs ที่ควรพิจารณา hold: bilious/bloody GR, distended tight abdomen, absent bowel sounds, bloody stool, clinical deterioration — ไม่ใช่ 'GR 3 mL' เดียวๆ.
Q: อธิบาย energy balance ใน PN: ratio glucose:lipid ที่ optimal และทำไมไม่ควร overload glucose?

ตอบ: Optimal nonprotein caloric ratio: carbohydrate:lipid ≈ 60:40 (mimics breast milk fat; maximal protein retention; minimal excess energy expenditure). Providing excess glucose: ↑ glucose oxidation beyond capacity → lactic acidosis; ↑ fat synthesis (energy wasteful); ↑ CO2 production → ↑ respiratory load. Glucose >12.5%: central access only. Providing excessive PN calories does NOT ↑ growth rate — ปริมาณ protein คือ primary determinant ของ protein accretion.
Q: DoMINO trial: อะไรคือ result จริงๆ และ implication ต่อ donor milk policy?

ตอบ: O'Connor et al JAMA 2016: 363 VLBW, donor HM vs preterm formula as supplement to maternal milk. Primary outcome (BSID-III cognitive mean score): no statistically significant difference. HOWEVER: infants randomized to donor milk more likely to score <85 on BSID-III — suggests possible signal. AAP (Parker Pediatrics 2021): still recommends donor HM when maternal milk unavailable (↓ NEC benefit clear). Key message: donor milk is NOT equivalent to maternal milk for NDO; fortification + tracking essential; NICHD NRN trial (NCT01534481) ongoing for NDO outcomes — results awaited.

Day 140 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.43
บทต่อไป: Chapter 43 — Support for the Family. เตรียมตัว: family-centered care (FCC) principles ใน NICU — นิยาม, evidence base, implementation; parental stress and bonding — parental roles in NICU, kangaroo mother care (KMC), skin-to-skin contact; communication with families: difficult conversations, delivering bad news, shared decision-making; sibling relationships และ discharge planning; cultural competency ใน NICU settings; mental health support for parents — PTSD, depression, anxiety rates ใน NICU parents; Thai NICU context: ข้อจำกัดในการเข้าเยี่ยมและ family participation — เรื่องที่ neonatologist ต้องเป็นผู้นำใน system change.
